boundary: PCEA runtime remains symmetric and state-synchronized. UCNS entries here are proving-ground candidates, attack records, or context layers; they are not shipped public-key claims.

candidates.claim: Use out-of-band provisioned entropy to synchronize PCEA peers, then add ratcheting, key separation, and transcript-bound authentication.
candidates.next_attack: Build a non-runtime ratcheted-session prototype and test replay, transcript mismatch, rollback-safe decrypt failure, and resynchronization metadata leakage.

candidates.claim: Use a reviewed standard KEM or Diffie-Hellman layer to establish the initial PCEA shared secret when cold-start communication is required.
candidates.next_attack: Write a dependency-policy decision record before code; require external test vectors and negative tests if an implementation is ever attempted.

candidates.claim: Use canonical UCNS objects as identity, capability, session, and transcript context hashed into PCEA key derivation and MAC associated data; UCNS is not the source of secrecy.
candidates.next_attack: Define canonical UCNS transcript serialization and test that every identity/session/capability change changes derived keys and tags.

candidates.claim: Small catalogue-complete UCNS domains can hide key material.
known_attacks: factor_search_v08 recovers random products in the frozen oracle domain
candidates.next_attack: None for promotion; keep as a negative control proving the harness breaks known-bad domains.

candidates.claim: Holding the public carrier fixed while hiding angle positions, faces, or payload structure can hide the private factor.
known_attacks: factor_search_v08 recovers recomposing factorizations at held-fixed carrier
non-unique recomposing factors are sufficient when the shared secret derives from a factorization
candidates.next_attack: Do not revive without a new public map that defeats factorization itself, not merely the carrier projection.

candidates.claim: Publishing an ordered product with three or more factors can smear the private-factor boundary enough to hide a designated private factor.
known_attacks: recursive peeling recovers the designated factor at catastrophic rates
factor-count sweep shows no protective decay across 2..6 factors
full-space non-uniqueness does not create secrecy
candidates.next_attack: Only revisit via hidden composition order or a key-space-restricted quotient model with explicit sublinear-search analysis.

candidates.claim: A private factor can be effectively unique inside a finite key space even when full-space factorizations are non-unique.
private_material: key-space member that left-divides the product
known_attacks: possible recursive-payload quotient or algebraic divisor shortcuts
candidates.next_attack: Search for sublinear quotient-guided attacks exploiting recursive payload quotient structure, algebraic divisor relations, or composition-order leakage.

candidates.claim: Publishing set-project(secret multiply base) defeats quotient inversion while preserving honest two-sided agreement.
known_attacks: Minkowski set-basis recovery solves the projected action
attack cost beats brute force asymptotically
candidates.next_attack: Closed as a KEM candidate; any future lossy map must defeat both quotient inversion and set-basis inversion.

candidates.claim: Use PCEA-advanced private gonals as a synchronized state/token communication layer for parties sharing an instantiation seed.
candidates.next_attack: Specify and test the 53-to-32 bridge with chosen-plaintext, known-plaintext, reuse-group, collision-rate, and legitimate-recovery probes before any shipped gonal cipher.

candidates.claim: Use UCNS public objects as commitments to future state, capabilities, or transcript checkpoints, with standard-library hashing for binding.
private_material: shared session secret when commitments are transcript-bound
candidates.next_attack: Prototype commit/open transcripts and test binding under UCNS canonical equivalence plus hiding against low-entropy brute force.
